home.title: School Re-Entry Guide
home.subtitle: Panama
home.description: A comprehensive guide for students and families returning to school after a health-related absence, or integrating into the educational system for the first time.
home.audience: For elementary and middle school students and their families
home.disclaimer: NOTE: This guide is for reference only. Please verify current requirements directly with MEDUCA or your school.

checklist.title: Checklist
checklist.desc: Personalized steps based on your situation

health.title: Health Documentation
health.desc: Medical certificates and school health management

faq.title: FAQ
faq.desc: Answers to the most common questions

docs.title: Documents & Forms
docs.desc: Letter templates and official form links

checklist.title: Personalized Checklist
checklist.subtitle: Select your situation to see the steps you need to follow

situations.newEnrollment: New enrollment due to health reasons

checklist.readyDesc: You have marked all steps. Save your personalized list as PDF.

newEnrollment.detail: Must state that the student is fit to attend classes. Can be issued by a private physician, CSS, or MINSA.
newEnrollment.where: CSS, MINSA, or private physician (with official seal and license number)

newEnrollment.text: Get birth certificate (original and certified copy)
newEnrollment.detail: An original and certified copy are required. Available in person or online at the Civil Registry.
The student's ID or passport. If the minor does not have an ID, present the guardian's.
Must be recent, with a light background and on photographic paper. Many pharmacies offer this service.
The vaccination card must be current according to the national MINSA vaccination schedule.

newEnrollment.text: Identify the nearest school with available spots
newEnrollment.detail: Call ahead to confirm available spots and the assigned school zone. MEDUCA can provide guidance.
newEnrollment.where: Regional MEDUCA office or directly at the school

newEnrollment.text: Visit the school office with all documents
newEnrollment.detail: Bring all documents organized. The office may request additional copies.

newEnrollment.text: Request a meeting with the school counselor
newEnrollment.detail: The counselor will assess the student's needs and coordinate necessary support.

newEnrollment.text: Inform the school about any special medical condition
newEnrollment.detail: Provide a detailed medical letter. If medications are required during school hours, deliver them to the health office.
The CSS School Insurance covers accidents occurring on school grounds during school hours.

reingreso.text: Obtain a medical discharge or fitness certificate
reingreso.detail: Issued by the treating physician. Must explicitly state the student is fit to return to classes.
reingreso.where: Treating physician or health center (CSS / MINSA)

reingreso.text: Formally request re-entry from the school principal
reingreso.detail: Submit a formal letter to the principal explaining the situation and requesting re-entry.

reingreso.text: Gather previous academic records (report cards, grades)
reingreso.detail: Request copies of report cards and grades from the period before the absence from the school office.

reingreso.text: Request an academic catch-up plan from teachers
reingreso.detail: Under MEDUCA guidelines, you have the right to a catch-up plan. Request it in writing from each teacher.
The counselor will assess the student's emotional and academic state to coordinate necessary support.

reingreso.text: Inform school health staff about the medical condition
reingreso.detail: Inform about medications, physical restrictions, or any special supervision required.

reingreso.text: Update emergency contact information at the school
reingreso.detail: Verify and update phone number, alternative contact, and address at the school office.

reingreso.text: Emotionally prepare the student for the return
reingreso.detail: Talk with the student about their fears. The school counselor can provide professional support.

reingreso.text: Coordinate with teachers about necessary accommodations
reingreso.detail: You can request curricular adaptations in writing. Teachers are required to consider the situation.

reingreso.text: Verify that school insurance is active
reingreso.detail: Confirm with CSS or the school office that the school insurance is active before the first day.

refugee.text: Contact ONPAR for assistance and documentation
refugee.detail: ONPAR provides guidance on the full process and can provide or help obtain refugee documentation.
Any document works: refugee/asylum seeker card, passport, or birth certificate.
refugee.where: ONPAR or UNHCR if you have no documents at all

refugee.text: Gather academic records from country of origin if available
refugee.detail: Grades, diplomas, transcripts. If unavailable, a proficiency test can be requested instead.
refugee.where: If unavailable, contact MEDUCA or ONPAR for alternatives

refugee.text: Visit the nearest school and request enrollment
refugee.detail: By law (Decree 1225/2015), no school may deny enrollment due to lack of documents.
refugee.where: Nearest public school in your residential zone

refugee.text: Request a proficiency test if no academic documents available
refugee.detail: The school can administer a test to determine the appropriate educational level.

refugee.text: Begin study validation process at MEDUCA
refugee.detail: Foreign documents must be apostilled or legalized and translated into Spanish before submission.

refugee.text: Contact UNHCR Panama if you encounter access barriers
refugee.detail: If a school denies access without legal justification, UNHCR can intervene and support the case.

refugee.text: Request interpreter or cultural mediator support
refugee.detail: UNHCR and some NGOs offer cultural mediators and language interpreters at no cost.
refugee.where: UNHCR, Fe y Alegría, Cáritas Panamá

refugee.text: Enroll in Spanish language support programs
refugee.detail: Spanish proficiency is key for integration. Free programs are available.

refugee.text: Connect with school counselor for integration support
refugee.detail: The counselor can help facilitate integration with classmates, teachers, and school culture.

admin.title: Administrative Process
admin.subtitle: Documents and procedures for enrollment and re-entry

items.name: Birth certificate
items.detail: Original and certified copy. Obtainable at the Civil Registry.

items.name: Health certificate
items.detail: Issued by a CSS, MINSA, or private physician. Must indicate fitness to attend classes.

items.name: ID or passport
items.detail: Student's ID or passport. If a minor without ID, present the guardian's.

items.name: Passport-size photos
items.detail: 2 recent photographs with a light background.

items.name: Vaccination card
items.detail: Up to date according to the national MINSA vaccination schedule.

items.name: Guardian's affidavit
items.detail: Required by some schools. May be requested before a notary.

steps.title: Process Steps
items: Verify the enrollment period with the school (usually January–February for the following school year)
Go to the school office with all documents
Fill out the official enrollment form
Request a meeting with the school counselor to plan the integration
Inform about any special medical condition or educational needs

items.name: Medical discharge certificate
items.detail: Issued by the treating physician, indicating the student is fit to return to classes.

items.name: Formal re-entry request
items.detail: Letter addressed to the school principal explaining the situation and requesting re-entry.

items.name: Academic history
items.detail: Report cards and grades from the period prior to the absence.

items.name: Absence justification
items.detail: Medical documentation supporting the period of absence.

steps.title: Process Steps
items: Contact the school before the return to notify the return date
Present the medical discharge certificate at the school office
Meet with the principal and school counselor
Request an academic catch-up plan from each subject teacher
Coordinate necessary accommodations based on the health condition

items.name: MEDUCA
items.role: Ministry of Education — governing body of the educational system

items.name: Civil Registry
items.role: Issuance of birth certificates and national IDs

items.name: CSS
items.role: Social Security Fund — health certificates and school insurance

items.name: MINSA
items.role: Ministry of Health — vaccination cards and primary care

denial.title: What to do if the school refuses enrollment?
denial.desc: Every child in Panama has a constitutional right to education. If a public school refuses an enrollment request without valid legal justification, there is a formal complaint process available.
denial.legalNote: Law 34 of 1995 (Organic Education Law), Art. 8 — Education is a fundamental right. Any enrollment refusal must be legally justified and communicated in writing.

steps.title: Request written justification
steps.text: The school is required to provide the reasons for the refusal in writing. If they refuse to do so, document the date, time, and name of the official who denied enrollment.

steps.title: Check for missing documents
steps.text: Sometimes refusals are due to an incomplete file. Review MEDUCA's requirements and verify that all documents are in order.

steps.title: Contact the Regional MEDUCA Office
steps.text: File a formal written complaint at your regional MEDUCA office. MEDUCA can instruct the school to accept the student.

steps.title: Visit the Office of the Ombudsman
steps.text: The Ombudsman can mediate between the family and the school and issue binding recommendations. Call 500-3600, Mon–Fri, 8:00–16:00.

steps.title: File a complaint with the Administrative Attorney General
steps.text: If the refusal is arbitrary, the Administrative Attorney General can initiate a sanctioning process against the responsible official. Tel: 510-0700.

health.title: Health Documentation
health.subtitle: Managing medical needs in the school environment
health.certDesc: This document is issued by an authorized physician and confirms the student is fit to attend classes. It can be obtained at:
certSources: Private physician with official seal and license number

medicItems.text: Notify in writing the school administration and health office about any medication the student must take during school hours.
Provide a copy of the medical prescription to the health office with clear dosage and schedule instructions.
Medications must be kept at the school health office, not in the student's backpack, unless medically indicated otherwise.
Leave an updated contact number for medical emergencies during school hours.

health.chronicDesc: If the student has a chronic condition (diabetes, epilepsy, asthma, heart condition, etc.), it is recommended to:
chronicItems: Formally notify the school administration by letter or form
Provide an emergency action plan signed by the treating physician
Meet with the health office staff to explain warning signs
Request that the main teacher be informed on how to act
Consider requesting an Individualized Care Plan (ICA) if applicable
health.insuranceDesc: The CSS administers school insurance that covers accidents within the school. To activate or verify it:
insuranceItems: Request information at the school office or the nearest CSS polyclinic
Verify that the student is enrolled at the start of the school year
Insurance covers medical care for accidents occurring on school premises during school hours

emotional.title: Emotional Support
emotional.subtitle: Resources for the emotional well-being of students and families

studentItems.title: Talk to someone you trust
studentItems.text: Tell a teacher, counselor, or family member how you feel. You don't have to face this alone.

studentItems.title: Give yourself time to adjust
studentItems.text: It's normal to feel nervous about going back. Each day will get a little easier. Don't compare yourself to others.

studentItems.title: Reconnect with friends
studentItems.text: Look for classmates you remember fondly. A small conversation can make a big difference.

studentItems.title: Ask for help in class
studentItems.text: If you don't understand something, ask your teacher after class. Asking questions shows intelligence, not weakness.

studentItems.title: Celebrate small achievements
studentItems.text: Every day you attend, every assignment you submit, is progress. Recognize your own effort.

studentItems.title: Take care of your daily well-being
studentItems.text: Get enough sleep, eat on schedule, and do something you enjoy every day. Body and mind are connected.

parentItems.title: Listen actively
parentItems.text: Create a safe space where your child can express fears without judgment. Validate their emotions before offering solutions.

parentItems.title: Maintain communication with school
parentItems.text: Talk regularly with the counselor and teachers. School-family teamwork is essential.

parentItems.title: Warning signs
parentItems.text: Watch for: persistent mood changes, refusal to go to school, isolation, changes in sleep or appetite. Seek professional support if they persist.

parentItems.title: Routine at home
parentItems.text: Set regular schedules for sleeping, eating, and studying. Structure provides emotional security.

parentItems.title: Professional psychological support
parentItems.text: The school counselor can refer to mental health services. Seeking help is a sign of care, not weakness.

emotional.counselorDesc: Every public school in Panama has a school counselor. Their role is to:
counselorItems: Support the student's social and emotional integration
Mediate between teachers, students, and families
Identify needs for specialized psychological support
emotional.counselorAction: Request a meeting with the counselor on the first day back, or before if possible.

academic.title: Academic Support
academic.subtitle: Strategies to resume learning with confidence

gradingItems.title: Justifying absences
gradingItems.text: Submit the medical certificate at the school office. Absences justified for medical reasons allow for makeup evaluations.

gradingItems.title: Right to makeup
gradingItems.text: Under MEDUCA guidelines, students with justified absences have the right to make up missed exams and evaluations.

gradingItems.title: Makeup deadline
gradingItems.text: Coordinate with each teacher the deadlines for submitting pending work and taking makeup evaluations.

academic.planeDesc: If the student has been absent for an extended period, they have the right to request a personalized plan:
planeItems: Request it formally from the school principal
Each teacher defines the priority content to recover
A realistic recovery schedule is established
The counselor supervises the integration process

resourceItems.name: MEDUCA Digital Portal
resourceItems.text: Official platform with educational resources by level and subject.

resourceItems.name: Educational TV channel
resourceItems.text: MEDUCA broadcasts educational content on television. Check the schedule.

resourceItems.name: School library
resourceItems.text: Request access to textbooks on loan through the school office.

resourceItems.name: Study groups
resourceItems.text: Ask the counselor to connect you with classmates for collaborative study groups.

tipItems: Start with subjects you find easier to rebuild confidence
Establish a regular study schedule at home (30–60 min per day as a starting point)
Don't try to catch up on everything at once — prioritizing is key
Communicate any comprehension barriers to teachers without delay

refugee.title: Guide for Refugee and Migrant Students
refugee.subtitle: Rights, procedures, and support resources in Panama
refugee.rightsDesc: In Panama, all children, regardless of their immigration status, have the right to access free public education.

rights.text: Executive Decree No. 1225 of 2015 guarantees access to the educational system for refugee and asylum-seeking children, even without complete documentation.
Primary and basic education is free and compulsory for all children aged 4 to 15 in public schools.
No school may deny enrollment to a child solely because they lack documents from their country of origin.

refugee.withoutDocsTitle: If You Don't Have Academic Documents

withoutDocsItems.title: Proficiency test
withoutDocsItems.text: If you have no prior academic records, the school can administer a proficiency test to determine the appropriate educational level.

withoutDocsItems.title: Alternative documents
withoutDocsItems.text: Present any available document: refugee/asylum seeker card issued by ONPAR, passport, birth certificate, or any identification.

withoutDocsItems.title: UNHCR support
withoutDocsItems.text: If you face access barriers, contact UNHCR (ACNUR) Panama. They are experienced in advocating for the educational rights of refugee families.

refugee.withDocsTitle: If You Have Academic Documents from Abroad

withDocsItems.title: Apostille or consular legalization
withDocsItems.text: Documents from the country of origin must be apostilled or legalized through the Panamanian consulate in that country, followed by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Panama.

withDocsItems.title: Official translation
withDocsItems.text: If documents are not in Spanish, they must be translated by an authorized Public Translator in Panama.

withDocsItems.title: Validation at MEDUCA
withDocsItems.text: Submit legalized and translated documents to MEDUCA's Study Validation Office for official recognition of the educational level.

languageItems: If Spanish is not the primary language, ask the counselor for linguistic mediation support
Community language support programs exist — consult UNHCR or local NGOs
Teachers can adapt assessments during the language adjustment period

orgs.name: ONPAR
orgs.fullName: National Office for the Attention of Refugees
orgs.role: First point of contact for documentation and refugee rights in Panama

orgs.name: ACNUR / UNHCR
orgs.fullName: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees — Panama
orgs.role: Legal protection, advocacy, and resources for refugee families

orgs.name: MEDUCA
orgs.role: Study validation process and access to the educational system

denial.title: What to do if the school denies access?
denial.desc: Despite legal protections, some refugee students face barriers when trying to enroll. If this happens, follow these steps:
denial.legalNote: Executive Decree No. 1225 of 2015 — No public school in Panama may deny enrollment to a refugee child or asylum seeker solely due to lack of documents. Such denial is illegal and subject to sanctions.

steps.title: Assert your rights — state them clearly
steps.text: Inform the school principal that Decree 1225 of 2015 guarantees access to education regardless of immigration status. Many officials respond when they see the family knows their rights.

steps.title: Demand written justification for the refusal
steps.text: Ask for the legal grounds for the refusal in writing, with the official's name and signature. This is required for any subsequent complaint.

steps.title: Contact UNHCR immediately
steps.text: UNHCR has the capacity for direct intervention and experience resolving these cases. They are your most effective ally. Tel: 297-0970 / acnur.org/panama

steps.title: Contact ONPAR
steps.text: ONPAR can issue provisional documentation and communicate directly with the school or MEDUCA to resolve access. Tel: 507-9500

steps.title: File a complaint with the Office of the Ombudsman
steps.text: The Ombudsman can issue resolutions requiring the school to accept the student. This service is free and does not require a lawyer. Tel: 500-3600

contacts.name: ACNUR / UNHCR
contacts.role: Intervention in refugee school access denials

resources.title: Resource Directory
resources.subtitle: Institutions and organizations that can help you
resources.disclaimer: NOTE: Contact details may be updated. Verify information directly with each institution.

institutions.name: MEDUCA
institutions.description: Governing body of the educational system. Enrollment, study validation, education policies, complaint handling.

institutions.name: Civil Registry
institutions.description: Birth certificates, national identity cards, apostilles of official documents.

institutions.name: Immigration
institutions.description: Residence permits, immigration status, documentation for foreigners in Panama.

institutions.name: MIDES
institutions.description: Social assistance programs, support for vulnerable families, subsidies and scholarships.

institutions.name: MINSA
institutions.description: Health certificates, vaccination cards, regional health centers, school health programs.

institutions.name: CSS
institutions.description: Medical care for insured individuals, polyclinics, school insurance, pediatric specialties.

institutions.name: Hospital del Niño
institutions.fullName: Children's Hospital Dr. José Renán Esquivel
institutions.description: Pediatric reference hospital. Care for children with complex medical conditions.

institutions.name: ONPAR
institutions.fullName: National Office for the Attention of Refugees
institutions.description: Documentation and services for asylum seekers and refugees in Panama. Refugee identity card.

institutions.name: ACNUR / UNHCR
institutions.fullName: UN High Commissioner for Refugees — Panama
institutions.description: Legal protection, advocacy and resources for refugee families. Intervenes when access to education is denied.

institutions.name: IOM
institutions.description: Assistance to migrants, social integration, documentation and guidance on rights.

institutions.name: Fe y Alegría
institutions.description: Network of popular education and social promotion. School support for vulnerable communities and immigrants.

institutions.name: Cáritas Panamá
institutions.description: Humanitarian assistance, support for migrant and refugee families, cultural mediation.

institutions.name: Red Cross
institutions.description: Humanitarian aid, first aid, support in emergency and displacement situations.

institutions.name: SENNIAF
institutions.fullName: National Secretariat for Children, Adolescents and Family
institutions.description: Protection of children's and adolescents' rights. Free hotline for reports and emergencies.

institutions.name: Ombudsman
institutions.fullName: Office of the Ombudsman of Panama
institutions.description: Defense of fundamental rights, including the right to education. Accepts complaints about enrollment denials.

institutions.name: Procuraduria Admin.
institutions.fullName: Office of the Administrative Attorney General
institutions.description: Receives complaints against public institutions, including schools that deny access without legal justification.

institutions.name: SNE 9-1-1
institutions.description: National emergency line. Police, fire department, ambulances. Available throughout Panama.

institutions.name: MIDES Helpline 182
institutions.description: Support for vulnerable families, domestic violence, children at risk, and social emergencies.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Answers to the most common questions about school re-entry in Panama

items.q: Can a school reject my child's enrollment?
items.a: No. Education is a constitutional right in Panama (Ley 34 of 1995). No public school can refuse to enroll a child without valid written legal justification. If you face rejection, request the reason in writing and contact your regional MEDUCA office (Tel: 511-4400).

items.q: How long does the enrollment process take?
items.a: With all documents complete, the secretaría typically processes enrollment in 1–3 business days. Cases involving incomplete documentation or special circumstances (refugees, complex medical conditions) may take 1–2 weeks with MEDUCA involvement.

items.q: Do required documents have an expiration date?
items.a: Medical fitness certificates are generally valid for 6 months. Birth certificates don't expire, but some schools request recent certified copies. Vaccination cards must be current per the MINSA national schedule.

items.q: Must all documents be in Spanish?
items.a: Yes. All documents submitted to Panamanian educational institutions must be in Spanish. Foreign-language documents must be officially translated by an authorized Traductor Público (public translator) in Panama before submission.

items.q: Where do I get a medical fitness certificate?
items.a: You can get one at any MINSA health center (free of charge), at a CSS polyclinic if the child or a family member is insured, or from a private doctor with an official registration number from the Colegio Médico de Panamá.

items.q: What if my child doesn't have a national ID (cédula)?
items.a: For minors without a cédula, the birth certificate plus the parent or legal guardian's ID are accepted. The student's own ID is not a mandatory requirement for initial enrollment at most schools.

items.q: When is the official enrollment period in Panama?
items.a: Panama's school year runs February to December. Enrollment for the following year typically opens in January–February. Contact the school before January to confirm their specific dates, as they may vary by institution.

items.q: Does my child have to attend the school in our zone?
items.a: Public schools assign spots by residential zone. If your zone school has no vacancies, MEDUCA can guide you to alternatives. Call ahead to confirm availability before arriving with documents.

items.q: Will my child lose grades from the absence period?
items.a: Not necessarily. Medically justified absences allow the student to recover exams and evaluated activities. The student has the right to a 'plan de nivelación' (academic recovery plan) that each teacher must prepare, outlining priority content and a reasonable recovery timeline.

items.q: How soon must the medical discharge certificate be submitted?
items.a: Submit it as soon as possible—ideally on the first day back. Most schools require it within the first week of return. Provide one copy to the secretaría and one to the school nurse (enfermería) for the student's health file.
Yes. No rule requires waiting for a new academic period. The student may return at any time with the appropriate medical certificate, and the school is obligated to accept them.

items.q: What if the absence was very long (several months)?
items.a: For extended absences, the school counselor and teachers must coordinate a more comprehensive recovery plan. In exceptional cases, the faculty council may assess whether grade repetition or intensive support is more appropriate. Families have the right to actively participate in that decision.

items.q: Can my child enter school without any documents?
items.a: Yes. Executive Decree No. 1225 of 2015 guarantees access to public education regardless of immigration status or lack of documents from the country of origin. No public school can legally deny this. If refused, contact ACNUR immediately (Tel: 297-0970).

items.q: What is the 'prueba de suficiencia' (sufficiency test) and how does it work?
items.a: It is an assessment the school uses when a student has no academic records from abroad. It evaluates reading, writing, and basic math to determine the appropriate grade level. It is a placement tool—not a pass/fail exam—with no negative academic consequences.

items.q: Which organization can help me most quickly?
items.a: ONPAR (Tel: 507-9500) is the official first point of contact for refugee documentation in Panama. ACNUR/UNHCR (Tel: 297-0970) can directly intervene with schools or MEDUCA if access is blocked. Both services are completely free of charge.

items.q: Can the school demand immigration documents?
items.a: Regarding enrollment access, no. Schools cannot make enrollment conditional on immigration status. They may, however, request any available alternative identification such as a refugee or asylum-seeker card issued by ONPAR, or any other identification document you have.

items.q: How are foreign academic credentials validated?
items.a: Through MEDUCA's Revalidación de Estudios office. Steps: 1) Have documents apostilled or legalized through the Panamanian consulate in the country of origin, 2) Translate them to Spanish by an authorized Traductor Público in Panama, 3) Submit to MEDUCA for official recognition. The process typically takes several weeks.

footer.disclaimer: This guide is for reference only. Please verify current requirements directly with MEDUCA or your school.
